meta.title: Methodology
meta.description: Scientific methodology behind the Asteroid Impact Simulator — formulas, citations, and limitations.

overview.p1: The Asteroid Impact Simulator calculates the regional environmental consequences of a meteoroid impact on Earth using the physics engine derived from Collins, Melosh & Marcus (2005).
overview.p2: Given an asteroid's diameter, density, impact velocity, and entry angle, the simulator estimates crater size, airburst altitude, thermal radiation, air blast overpressure, and seismic magnitude.
overview.accuracy: Results are accurate to within ±5% for typical asteroid parameters, validated against the Collins et al. (2005) golden test suite.

kineticEnergy.p1: The kinetic energy of the impactor is the primary driver of all subsequent effects. It is computed from the asteroid's mass and velocity.
kineticEnergy.p2: Mass is derived from the spherical volume approximation and the user-selected density (ranging from 1000 kg/m³ for icy bodies to 8000 kg/m³ for iron meteorites).

atmosphericEntry.p1: As the asteroid enters the atmosphere, it experiences ablation and deceleration. The simulator uses the simplified ablation model from Collins et al. (2005) Appendix A.
atmosphericEntry.p2: If the asteroid's strength is insufficient to survive atmospheric entry, it bursts at an altitude calculated by the following scaling relation:

craterDiameter.p1: The transient crater diameter follows the Schmidt-Housen-Holsapple scaling law for planetary impacts, relating energy, target density, and gravity.
craterDiameter.p2: The final crater diameter is approximately 1.25× the transient diameter for simple craters, or follows complex crater morphometry for larger events.

fireballThermal.p1: The fireball radius scales with the cube root of impact energy. Thermal radiation flux at a given distance follows an inverse-square law with an empirical constant K.
fireballThermal.p2: Third-degree burn threshold (~10 cal/cm²) is used to estimate the thermal hazard radius.

airBlast.p1: Air blast overpressure is calculated using Glasstone & Dolan (1977) scaling laws adapted for impact events. The simulator reports four standard damage thresholds:
airBlast.p2: Overpressure decreases with distance from the epicenter. Structural damage begins at ~1 psi; total destruction occurs above ~20 psi.
airBlast.psi1: 1 psi — Window shattering, minor structural damage

seismic.p1: The seismic moment magnitude is estimated from the impact energy using the empirical relation from Melosh (1989), derived from lunar and terrestrial impact data.
seismic.p2: This is a conservative estimate; actual ground motion depends strongly on local geology and coupling efficiency.

civilizationRisk.p1: The simulator classifies impact consequences into a 6-tier scale ranging from local effects to global extinction risk.
civilizationRisk.p2: Events exceeding ~10²¹ J (roughly 100 km diameter) approach the threshold for global mass extinction, based on the Chicxulub impact energy estimate.
civilizationRisk.torino: The Torino Scale (0–10) is referenced for public communication of impact hazard.
